content.text: Armor is a clock you wear. Every fight winds it down; every invoice winds it back up.

Armor brings three numbers and a ceiling: Value (hardness — sets the bounce gate), Absorb (padding — routes punch-through away from flesh), Armor HP (the shell's own health bar), and an Evasion cap (heavier shells cap how high your Evasion can sit). How damage moves through all four is the Shell Clock, and it lives on Attacks & the Shell Clock. This page owns the ladder.
The ladder
The Evasion formula and how the cap bites live on Attacks & the Shell Clock. “—” means uncapped.
Every armor has 2 mod slots — the armor mod catalog lives on Mods.
Reading the ladder
Every tier answers a different question.
Contractor Standard and Corp Tactical are where the light blade's grind lane lives — medium shells are what knives chew through.
Raid Mail is a purchasable, knowable threshold: the grind dies at Value 5. Flurries stop mattering here.
Heavy Plate is a heavy two-hander's dedicated project — heavy weapons punch medium shells and crack heavy ones.
Siege Rig means bring Impact or a breach plan. Impact opens anything; Pierce never plays the armor game.
Breaking

At 0 Armor HP the shell breaks. All protection is gone — Value and Absorb stop existing, and until it is repaired the wearer takes hits as flesh: straight damage dice, no gate, no router (see Attacks & the Shell Clock). Breaking is catastrophic by design, and the repair economy fires the moment it happens.
The Spall Liner mod softens the cliff for one round (see Mods).

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: What happens to the Evasion cap when a shell breaks is unruled. The Spall Liner mod (“keep its EV cap and 1 Absorb until the end of the next round”) implies the cap is normally lost on break — but no doc states the baseline.
Field repair

Field repair is what happens between fights, at a rest stop, with paste and field tools. Baseline field repair risks the permanent-consequence roll. Repair Paste restores 1d6 Armor HP and carries that risk (see Consumables & Ammo). Two things wave it, each in its own lane: Shell Splint (see Systems) and Warranty Work (see Tinkerer).

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: The permanent-consequence roll is referenced everywhere field repair appears — Repair Paste risks it, rest stops run field repair “with its existing permanent-consequence rules” — but no Phase 7 doc prints the roll itself: no procedure, no consequence table. The risk exists; its mechanics do not.
The repair bill

Repair pricing scales off Armor HP restored — the deeper the crack, the longer the invoice. A broken shell is the expensive case: that is the reforge economy, and it fires every time a shell hits zero.

You can repair your own gear at material cost if you have the capability, the components, and the calendar — the Material Cost Law lives with the GM's economy pages. The person who does this for a living is the Tinkerer (see Tinkerer).

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: No repair or reforge price table exists in the Phase 7 docs — only the scaling principle (price scales with Armor HP restored). The numbers land with the economy pass.
Repair invoices itemize by hit point restored. Many contractors find reviewing them instructive: the shell remembers exactly what you got wrong, and so does billing.
